prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: Throughput refers to the amount of data that can be processed in a given time frame, often measured in gigabytes per second (GB/s). It represents the total amount of data that can be transferred from the client to the server in a short period of time. Latency, on the other hand, is the time it takes for data to be processed by the inference model, typically measured in milliseconds (ms). It is the time it takes for the data to reach the model from the client or the server, and it can be affected by various factors such as network latency, server load, or hardware limitations.
isolation_strategy: Process isolation using taskset: inference pinned to core 0, stress-ng pinned to core 1

prompt: Explain the difference between throughput and latency in AI inference using one short paragraph.
reference_for_bertscore: Latency measures how long one inference takes from input to output, while throughput measures how many tokens or requests are processed per unit of time. A system can have high throughput but still be unsuitable for real-time use if individual responses arrive too late.
output: Throughput refers to the amount of data processed per unit of time, while latency refers to the time it takes to process the data. In AI inference, throughput measures the speed at which the AI model can process data, while latency measures the time it takes to complete the processing of data. In many cases, high throughput and low latency are desired, but not all systems require them simultaneously. In some cases, a trade-off may need to be made between throughput and latency to achieve optimal performance.
